ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clear, concrete combination of CTC and attention-based end-to-end ASR by attaching a CTC objective to the attention encoder and fusing CTC+attention during decoding — a non-trivial methodological extension that directly targets known alignment failure modes (repetitions/deletions). The authors differentiate their approach from prior CTC-only and attention-only systems and validate on CSJ and HKUST, showing competitive results without linguistic resources. From an experimental-methods perspective this is a notable and informative novelty rather than a trivial remix of existing techniques, though it builds on well-established components rather than introducing an entirely new paradigm. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and practically useful idea: jointly training a CTC objective with an attention-based encoder and using combined CTC-attention scores during decoding to mitigate attention irregularities (repetitions/deletions). This formulation clearly differentiates itself from pure attention or pure CTC approaches by using CTC as an alignment regularizer and provides a non-trivial extension to end-to-end ASR, demonstrated on CSJ and HKUST. While the approach combines established components (CTC and attention), the particular integration and decoding strategy would be informative to ASR experts and goes beyond a straightforward combination of known techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a clear, concrete combination of CTC and attention-based end-to-end ASR by attaching a CTC objective to the attention encoder and fusing CTC+attention during decoding — a non-trivial methodological extension that directly targets known alignment failure modes (repetitions/deletions). The authors differentiate their approach from prior CTC-only and attention-only systems and validate on CSJ and HKUST, showing competitive results without linguistic resources. From an experimental-methods perspective this is a notable and informative novelty rather than a trivial remix of existing techniques, though it builds on well-established components rather than introducing an entirely new paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and practically useful idea: jointly training a CTC objective with an attention-based encoder and using combined CTC-attention scores during decoding to mitigate attention irregularities (repetitions/deletions). This formulation clearly differentiates itself from pure attention or pure CTC approaches by using CTC as an alignment regularizer and provides a non-trivial extension to end-to-end ASR, demonstrated on CSJ and HKUST. While the approach combines established components (CTC and attention), the particular integration and decoding strategy would be informative to ASR experts and goes beyond a straightforward combination of known techniques.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (joint CTC-attention training and combined decoding) are stated clearly early; writing quality and related work coverage are good. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples seem effective for communicating experiments on CSJ and HKUST. A notable presentation gap is that evaluation metrics are not specified in the provided summary (and code release is not mentioned), which limits reproducibility and clarity of experimental reporting. These are minor but important issues for readers trying to reproduce or compare results. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear progression from motivation to method, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions (joint CTC‑attention training and decoding to mitigate alignment issues) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is good. However, one clarity gap is that evaluation metrics are not specified in the provided summary, which hampers understanding of the reported results; also a few experimental details (e.g., code availability) are not mentioned, so minor presentation improvements would help reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (joint CTC-attention training and combined decoding) are stated clearly early; writing quality and related work coverage are good. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples seem effective for communicating experiments on CSJ and HKUST. A notable presentation gap is that evaluation metrics are not specified in the provided summary (and code release is not mentioned), which limits reproducibility and clarity of experimental reporting. These are minor but important issues for readers trying to reproduce or compare results.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear progression from motivation to method, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions (joint CTC‑attention training and decoding to mitigate alignment issues) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is good. However, one clarity gap is that evaluation metrics are not specified in the provided summary, which hampers understanding of the reported results; also a few experimental details (e.g., code availability) are not mentioned, so minor presentation improvements would help reproducibility.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a clearly relevant NLP/ML topic—end-to-end speech recognition—by proposing a joint CTC–attention training and decoding strategy and evaluating it on standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) against strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC, TDNN with LF-MMI). From an experimental-methods perspective it largely targets the right audience: it reports hyperparameters, compares to conventional hybrid systems, and discusses limitations (not matching TDNN+LF-MMI), which makes it suitable for a top venue. My main remaining concern is reproducibility (code availability not mentioned) and the need for detailed variance/statistical analysis or multiple runs to fully meet top-tier experimental standards, preventing a perfect score. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a central, current problem in end-to-end ASR (combining CTC and attention to mitigate alignment errors) and demonstrates empirical gains on standard corpora (CSJ and HKUST) while comparing to strong baselines including hybrid Kaldi recipes and TDNN LF‑MMI. The methodology and evaluations follow community expectations (joint objective, decoding with combined scores, hyperparameters reported) and the claim of reducing reliance on linguistic resources has clear practical significance for multilingual and low-resource settings. Although it does not yet surpass TDNN with LF‑MMI, that limitation is acknowledged and does not reduce the paper’s relevance to this venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a clearly relevant NLP/ML topic—end-to-end speech recognition—by proposing a joint CTC–attention training and decoding strategy and evaluating it on standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) against strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC, TDNN with LF-MMI). From an experimental-methods perspective it largely targets the right audience: it reports hyperparameters, compares to conventional hybrid systems, and discusses limitations (not matching TDNN+LF-MMI), which makes it suitable for a top venue. My main remaining concern is reproducibility (code availability not mentioned) and the need for detailed variance/statistical analysis or multiple runs to fully meet top-tier experimental standards, preventing a perfect score.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a central, current problem in end-to-end ASR (combining CTC and attention to mitigate alignment errors) and demonstrates empirical gains on standard corpora (CSJ and HKUST) while comparing to strong baselines including hybrid Kaldi recipes and TDNN LF‑MMI. The methodology and evaluations follow community expectations (joint objective, decoding with combined scores, hyperparameters reported) and the claim of reducing reliance on linguistic resources has clear practical significance for multilingual and low-resource settings. Although it does not yet surpass TDNN with LF‑MMI, that limitation is acknowledged and does not reduce the paper’s relevance to this venue.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—unstable/irregular alignments in attention-only end-to-end ASR—by proposing a joint CTC-attention training and decoding scheme that demonstrably mitigates repetitions/deletions and achieves performance comparable to conventional hybrid systems on CSJ and HKUST. From my experimental-methods perspective, this is likely to change how practitioners combine alignment constraints with sequence models and will prompt follow-up work (e.g., ablations on weighting, decoding fusion, robustness across languages). It does not fully displace strong lattice-free sequence-discriminative TDNN results, and code/reproducibility artifacts are not mentioned, so impact is substantial but not transformative. | B(4): This paper targets an important, practical problem—the irregular alignments of attention-based end-to-end ASR and dependence on linguistic resources—by proposing a joint CTC–attention training and decoding scheme that uses CTC as an alignment regularizer. Empirically, it achieves hybrid-comparable performance on realistic Japanese (CSJ) and Mandarin (HKUST) benchmarks without requiring language-specific resources, which makes the approach attractive for multilingual and low-resource deployment. While it does not yet surpass LF‑MMI TDNNs, the method is likely to change practitioner choices and spawn follow-up work on hybrid loss/decoding strategies and alignment-aware architectures. Overall, the contribution should be widely adopted and opens clear new avenues for reducing reliance on lexicons and language models in end-to-end ASR.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—unstable/irregular alignments in attention-only end-to-end ASR—by proposing a joint CTC-attention training and decoding scheme that demonstrably mitigates repetitions/deletions and achieves performance comparable to conventional hybrid systems on CSJ and HKUST. From my experimental-methods perspective, this is likely to change how practitioners combine alignment constraints with sequence models and will prompt follow-up work (e.g., ablations on weighting, decoding fusion, robustness across languages). It does not fully displace strong lattice-free sequence-discriminative TDNN results, and code/reproducibility artifacts are not mentioned, so impact is substantial but not transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper targets an important, practical problem—the irregular alignments of attention-based end-to-end ASR and dependence on linguistic resources—by proposing a joint CTC–attention training and decoding scheme that uses CTC as an alignment regularizer. Empirically, it achieves hybrid-comparable performance on realistic Japanese (CSJ) and Mandarin (HKUST) benchmarks without requiring language-specific resources, which makes the approach attractive for multilingual and low-resource deployment. While it does not yet surpass LF‑MMI TDNNs, the method is likely to change practitioner choices and spawn follow-up work on hybrid loss/decoding strategies and alignment-aware architectures. Overall, the contribution should be widely adopted and opens clear new avenues for reducing reliance on lexicons and language models in end-to-end ASR.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper makes clear, testable claims about a joint CTC-attention model and supports them with experiments on two standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (DNN-hybrid, syllable CTC, TDNN). Mathematical formulation appears standard and complete for the joint objective, and the empirical results show the claimed mitigation of alignment errors and competitive performance. However, the experimental methodology lacks important reproducibility and robustness evidence: evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary, there is no mention of multiple runs/variance or statistical significance testing, and code/artifacts are not provided to verify matched hyperparameter and decoding protocols against baselines. Given these relatively minor but important gaps, the claims are credible but not fully validated to the degree I would require for a flawless technical endorsement. | B(4): The paper states clear, testable claims (joint CTC-attention reduces attention alignment errors and yields hybrid-competitive performance) and backs them with experiments on two standard corpora (CSJ and HKUST) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (DNN-hybrid, syllable-CTC, TDNN LF-MMI). There are no apparent mathematical errors in the described approach (CTC loss appended to attention encoder), and hyperparameters and related work are reported. Minor technical concerns remain: evaluation metrics and statistical significance testing are not specified in the summary, and the decoding combination of CTC and attention probabilities rests on an assumed complementarity that could use more ablation/analysis. These do not undermine the main experimental conclusions, so the work is technically sound with small gaps in experimental rigor.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes clear, testable claims about a joint CTC-attention model and supports them with experiments on two standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (DNN-hybrid, syllable CTC, TDNN). Mathematical formulation appears standard and complete for the joint objective, and the empirical results show the claimed mitigation of alignment errors and competitive performance. However, the experimental methodology lacks important reproducibility and robustness evidence: evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary, there is no mention of multiple runs/variance or statistical significance testing, and code/artifacts are not provided to verify matched hyperparameter and decoding protocols against baselines. Given these relatively minor but important gaps, the claims are credible but not fully validated to the degree I would require for a flawless technical endorsement.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims (joint CTC-attention reduces attention alignment errors and yields hybrid-competitive performance) and backs them with experiments on two standard corpora (CSJ and HKUST) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (DNN-hybrid, syllable-CTC, TDNN LF-MMI). There are no apparent mathematical errors in the described approach (CTC loss appended to attention encoder), and hyperparameters and related work are reported. Minor technical concerns remain: evaluation metrics and statistical significance testing are not specified in the summary, and the decoding combination of CTC and attention probabilities rests on an assumed complementarity that could use more ablation/analysis. These do not undermine the main experimental conclusions, so the work is technically sound with small gaps in experimental rigor.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against strong and relevant baselines (Kaldi DNN-hybrid, syllable-based CTC, and TDNN with LF sequence training) on standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST), and it explicitly acknowledges that TDNN+LF-MMI still outperforms the proposed method. From an experimental-methods perspective the comparisons appear well-chosen and related work is discussed thoroughly. However, the summary does not confirm whether all comparisons used identical evaluation metrics, lexica/LMs, or decoding conditions (and code/repro configs aren’t mentioned), so I cannot verify full fairness and reproducibility—hence not a perfect score. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and competitive baselines (Kaldi DNN-hybrid, syllable-based CTC, and TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training) on relevant benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST), and reports hyperparameters. It fairly acknowledges when a prior method (TDNN with LF-MMI) outperforms the proposal. The paper clearly explains how its joint CTC–attention approach differs and how CTC provides alignment constraints. Minor shortcomings are that evaluation metrics are not specified in the provided summary (so full fairness of numeric comparisons cannot be verified) and no mention of code/reproducibility, hence I rate it a strong but not fully comprehensive comparative study.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against strong and relevant baselines (Kaldi DNN-hybrid, syllable-based CTC, and TDNN with LF sequence training) on standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST), and it explicitly acknowledges that TDNN+LF-MMI still outperforms the proposed method. From an experimental-methods perspective the comparisons appear well-chosen and related work is discussed thoroughly. However, the summary does not confirm whether all comparisons used identical evaluation metrics, lexica/LMs, or decoding conditions (and code/repro configs aren’t mentioned), so I cannot verify full fairness and reproducibility—hence not a perfect score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and competitive baselines (Kaldi DNN-hybrid, syllable-based CTC, and TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training) on relevant benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST), and reports hyperparameters. It fairly acknowledges when a prior method (TDNN with LF-MMI) outperforms the proposal. The paper clearly explains how its joint CTC–attention approach differs and how CTC provides alignment constraints. Minor shortcomings are that evaluation metrics are not specified in the provided summary (so full fairness of numeric comparisons cannot be verified) and no mention of code/reproducibility, hence I rate it a strong but not fully comprehensive comparative study.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates the joint CTC–attention method on two standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and compares to strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, syllable-CTC, TDNN/lattice-free), and reports hyperparameters — which gives solid empirical support for the main claim that CTC guidance mitigates attention alignment errors and yields competitive performance. However, the summary does not indicate any ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contributions of (a) the CTC loss weight, (b) decoding fusion vs. training-only effects, or (c) variance across runs/initializations; those experiments are important to be convincing. The authors do discuss limitations (did not match TDNN LF-MMI) and cover related work thoroughly, but reproducibility is only partially satisfied (hyperparameters reported but code, training curves, compute budgets, and multiple seeds are not mentioned). Given these strengths and the missing ablations/repro details, I rate the experimental rigor as strong but with minor gaps. | B(4): The experiments cover two realistic languages (CSJ and HKUST) and compare to multiple strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, syllable CTC, TDNN with LF-MMI), which supports the main claims about mitigation of attention alignment errors. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are not clearly reported in the summary (e.g., varied CTC/attention weighting or decoding-only vs. training-only contributions), so some important analyses are missing. Related work is described as thorough and several limitations are explicitly discussed (failure to match TDNN LF-MMI, attention alignment issues, and CTC decoding constraints). Reproducibility is partly supported by reported hyperparameters, but absence of code and unspecified evaluation metrics reduce full reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates the joint CTC–attention method on two standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and compares to strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, syllable-CTC, TDNN/lattice-free), and reports hyperparameters — which gives solid empirical support for the main claim that CTC guidance mitigates attention alignment errors and yields competitive performance. However, the summary does not indicate any ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating the contributions of (a) the CTC loss weight, (b) decoding fusion vs. training-only effects, or (c) variance across runs/initializations; those experiments are important to be convincing. The authors do discuss limitations (did not match TDNN LF-MMI) and cover related work thoroughly, but reproducibility is only partially satisfied (hyperparameters reported but code, training curves, compute budgets, and multiple seeds are not mentioned). Given these strengths and the missing ablations/repro details, I rate the experimental rigor as strong but with minor gaps.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments cover two realistic languages (CSJ and HKUST) and compare to multiple strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, syllable CTC, TDNN with LF-MMI), which supports the main claims about mitigation of attention alignment errors. Ablation/sensitivity analyses are not clearly reported in the summary (e.g., varied CTC/attention weighting or decoding-only vs. training-only contributions), so some important analyses are missing. Related work is described as thorough and several limitations are explicitly discussed (failure to match TDNN LF-MMI, attention alignment issues, and CTC decoding constraints). Reproducibility is partly supported by reported hyperparameters, but absence of code and unspecified evaluation metrics reduce full reproducibility.

main_claims: Proposes a joint CTC-attention end-to-end ASR that leverages benefits of both CTC and attention during training and decoding.
The joint CTC-attention approach mitigates irregular alignment problems (e.g., repetitions and deletions) observed in attention-only ASR by using CTC-based alignment constraints.
Applied to Japanese and Mandarin benchmarks, the proposed method achieves performance comparable to conventional hybrid ASR systems without requiring linguistic resources.
_summary.methodology: Attach a CTC objective to an attention-based encoder network and train a joint CTC-attention network, using CTC to provide alignment constraints/regularization while decoding uses combined CTC-attention probabilities.
datasets: HKUST Mandarin Chinese conversational telephone speech recognition (MTS)
baselines: Syllable-based CTC with MAP decoding (Kanda et al.)
Time-delayed neural network (TDNN) with lattice-free sequence discriminative training (Povey et al.)
limitations: The proposed method did not reach the performance obtained by TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training.
Attention-based ASR can produce irregular alignments (repetitions and deletions) without CTC guidance.
CTC requires language models and graph-based decoding (except with huge training data).

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methods Specialist
reviewer_a.background: Senior research engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating ASR systems and deployed ML pipelines; strong experience in experimental design, hyperparameter tuning, and reproducible code release.
traits: insists on clear baselines, matched hyperparameters, and fair comparison protocols (e.g., same data, lexicon, language model use)
values ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each component (e.g., CTC loss, weighting, decoding fusion)
looks for statistical significance, variance reporting, and multiple runs or seeds to assess robustness
checks for sufficient implementation details and reproducibility artifacts (code, configs, training curves, model sizes, compute budget)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the experiments convincingly support the claims through rigorous comparisons, ablations, and reproducible reporting.

reviewer_b.name: Speech Recognition Systems Expert
reviewer_b.background: Tenured academic with a long track record in speech recognition research covering hybrid and end-to-end ASR, language resources, and multilingual evaluations; active in setting community benchmarks and best practices.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior CTC/attention and hybrid ASR work and how the contribution advances the field
attends to practical impact: whether the method reduces reliance on linguistic resources and its applicability across languages and low-resource settings
notices alignment and decoding behaviors (e.g., repetitions, deletions) and values qualitative analyses of error types and examples
considers scalability and deployment aspects such as latency, model complexity, and integration with language models or lexicons
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses the paper's originality and broader significance for the ASR community, weighing empirical gains against novelty and practical adoption potential.
